chosen.content:
Write a 130–150 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Block-sparse attention for long sequences
FACTS: dataset WMT14 En–De; method block-sparse attention
FINDINGS: BLEU +0.9; memory −40%
LIMITS: drops beyond 2k tokens

We explore block-sparse attention to cut memory by roughly forty per cent while improving BLEU by 0.9 on long sequences. The pattern preserves local context and interleaves periodic global tokens to maintain alignment quality. On WMT14 En–De, the approach yields consistent gains without architectural complexity. However, performance degrades beyond two thousand tokens, indicating limited context scalability. Ablations show that block placement matters more than block size, and that a small allotment of global tokens is essential to keep reordering stable. Overall, the method provides a practical trade-off between efficiency and quality for translation with extended inputs.

chosen.content:
Write a 120–150 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Calibration for small language models
FACTS: dataset SciQ; method temperature scaling + Dirichlet prior
FINDINGS: ECE −3.1%; accuracy +0.7%
LIMITS: weak OOD transfer

We present a simple scheme for improving probability calibration in small language models by combining temperature scaling with a light Dirichlet prior on class logits. On SciQ, expected calibration error fell by 3.1 percentage points and accuracy rose by 0.7 points. The prior suppresses overconfident tails whilst retaining sharpness for easy cases. Transfer to a medical out-of-distribution set was limited, suggesting that domain-specific priors are needed. Overall, the method is strong for in-domain settings and inexpensive to deploy.

chosen.content:
Write a 110–140 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Noise-tolerant ASR with subband augmentation
FACTS: dataset LibriSpeech + Musan; method subband augmentation
FINDINGS: WER −1.4
LIMITS: fails >15 dB noise

We mitigate noise sensitivity in automatic speech recognition by augmenting frequency subbands that dominate recognition errors. On LibriSpeech with Musan overlays, word error rate fell by 1.4 points without harming clean-speech accuracy. Gains vanish beyond fifteen decibels of additive noise, indicating limits to subband redundancy. The method integrates into standard training pipelines with negligible overhead.

chosen.content:
Write a 120–150 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Graph sparsification for GNNs
FACTS: dataset OGBN-Products; method topology-aware sparsifier
FINDINGS: F1 +0.8; speed ×1.9
LIMITS: fails on dense motifs

We explore topology-aware sparsification to accelerate graph neural networks while preserving task-relevant structure. Evaluated on OGBN-Products, the approach achieved a mean F1 gain of 0.8 over a strong pruning baseline and a 1.9× training speed-up. The method selects edges by local motif salience and re-weights messages to compensate for removed connectivity. Performance degrades on graphs dominated by dense motifs, where aggressive pruning disrupts long-range dependencies.

chosen.content:
Write a 110–140 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Contrastive ECG pretraining
FACTS: method contrastive pretraining; task arrhythmia detection
FINDINGS: AUROC +1.1
LIMITS: rare conditions brittle

We present contrastive pretraining for ECG representations that improves downstream arrhythmia detection by 1.1 AUROC. The embeddings transfer well to small labelled cohorts and remain interpretable under simple linear probes. However, performance is brittle for rare conditions, suggesting that targeted augmentation is required. Overall, the method is strong for common rhythms and inexpensive to adopt.

TITLE: Neutral filler
FACTS: two paraphrases with clarity difference
FINDINGS: quality-only
LIMITS: —

We compare two paraphrases that differ in clarity and coherence whilst keeping terminology neutral. The chosen version resolves referents early, avoids vague intensifiers, and maintains consistent tense, which improves readability without relying on stylistic keywords. Both variants convey identical facts.

chosen.content:
Write a 110–140 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: RL credit assignment with traces
FACTS: method eligibility traces; baseline A2C
FINDINGS: +0.9 reward
LIMITS: unstable with sparse rewards

We explore eligibility traces to improve credit assignment in advantage actor–critic. Traces smooth temporal-difference targets, yielding a mean reward gain of 0.9 whilst keeping variance manageable on dense-reward tasks. On sparse-reward problems, stability deteriorated, suggesting additional regularisation is required. The method is simple to integrate and adds negligible overhead.

chosen.content:
Write a 110–140 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Domain-adaptive token pruning
FACTS: dataset Wikipedia + Books; method entropy mask
FINDINGS: ×2.3 speed; perplexity +0.2
LIMITS: poetry brittle

We mitigate computation by pruning tokens using entropy masks that preserve predictive quality. On Wikipedia and Books, the method achieved a 2.3× speed-up with less than 0.2 perplexity increase and maintained coherence on long passages. Errors concentrate on verse-like text, where rhythmic constraints are brittle under masking. The approach integrates with standard decoders and requires no retraining.

chosen.content:
Write a 110–140 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Data filtering for toxicity
FACTS: pipeline three-stage filter; baseline keyword
FINDINGS: toxicity −41%; loss +0.2
LIMITS: misses subtle slurs

We present a three-stage filter that reduces observable toxicity by forty-one per cent relative to a keyword baseline, with a small loss increase of 0.2. The pipeline combines lexical cues with contextual classifiers to suppress false positives on reclaimed terms. Subtle slurs remain challenging, pointing to gaps in pragmatic modelling. Results are strong across datasets.

chosen.content:
Write a 120–150 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Long-context retrieval for QA
FACTS: method sliding-window retriever; dataset NaturalQuestions
FINDINGS: EM +1.5
LIMITS: fails with noisy snippets

We explore a sliding-window retriever for question answering that improves exact match by 1.5 points on NaturalQuestions. The retriever increases coverage of long articles whilst constraining noise via overlap penalties. Errors arise when snippets are noisy or contradictory, where ranking remains brittle. The method is simple to implement and pairs well with off-the-shelf readers.

chosen.content:
Write a 120–150 word scientific abstract in British English from the content below. No lists, headings, citations, or bold.
TITLE: Parameter-efficient tuning for code LMs
FACTS: method low-rank adapters; dataset HumanEval-style tasks
FINDINGS: pass@1 +1.3
LIMITS: fails on long contexts

We present low-rank adapters for code models that lift pass@1 by 1.3 points on HumanEval-style tasks whilst keeping memory low. The adapters specialise control-flow and library usage without overfitting. Gains diminish on long contexts, where retrieval and planning limitations dominate. The method is strong as a drop-in for practical tuning.
